content:
First observe that any “best” integer approximation must use only 2’s and 3’s.  Since 
\(\sum a_i=19\) and there are 7 terms, if we use \(k\) many 3’s and \(7-k\) many 2’s then
\[
3k+2(7-k)=19\;\Longrightarrow\;14+k=19\;\Longrightarrow\;k=5,
\]
so we must take exactly five 3’s and two 2’s.

For each \(i\) compute
\[
e_{i,2}=|a_i-2|,\quad e_{i,3}=|3-a_i|.
\]
A table of the seven values is

i | \(a_i\) | \(e_{i,2}=|a_i-2|\) | \(e_{i,3}=|3-a_i|\)
---|---|---|---
1 | 2.56 | 0.56 | 0.44  
2 | 2.61 | 0.61 | 0.39  
3 | 2.65 | 0.65 | 0.35  
4 | 2.71 | 0.71 | 0.29  
5 | 2.79 | 0.79 | 0.21  
6 | 2.82 | 0.82 | 0.18  
7 | 2.86 | 0.86 | 0.14  

We must choose two indices to round down to 2 (their errors are the \(e_{i,2}\) there) and the other five to round up to 3 (their errors are the \(e_{i,3}\) there), and then minimize
\[
M=\max\{\text{chosen }e_{i,2},\;\text{chosen }e_{j,3}\}.
\]
One checks that the two smallest \(e_{i,2}\) are at \(i=1,2\), namely 0.56 and 0.61.  If we make \(A_1=A_2=2\) and all the rest 3, then the largest error among the 3’s is
\(\max\{e_{3,3},\dots,e_{7,3}\}=0.35,\)
so
\[
M=\max(0.61,0.35)=0.61.
\]
No other choice of two 2’s gives a smaller maximum error.  Hence the minimal possible \(M\) is 0.61, and
\[
100M=61.
\]
\boxed{61}

message.content:
First observe that any “best” integer approximation must use only 2’s and 3’s.  Since 
\(\sum a_i=19\) and there are 7 terms, if we use \(k\) many 3’s and \(7-k\) many 2’s then
\[
3k+2(7-k)=19\;\Longrightarrow\;14+k=19\;\Longrightarrow\;k=5,
\]
so we must take exactly five 3’s and two 2’s.

For each \(i\) compute
\[
e_{i,2}=|a_i-2|,\quad e_{i,3}=|3-a_i|.
\]
A table of the seven values is

i | \(a_i\) | \(e_{i,2}=|a_i-2|\) | \(e_{i,3}=|3-a_i|\)
---|---|---|---
1 | 2.56 | 0.56 | 0.44  
2 | 2.61 | 0.61 | 0.39  
3 | 2.65 | 0.65 | 0.35  
4 | 2.71 | 0.71 | 0.29  
5 | 2.79 | 0.79 | 0.21  
6 | 2.82 | 0.82 | 0.18  
7 | 2.86 | 0.86 | 0.14  

We must choose two indices to round down to 2 (their errors are the \(e_{i,2}\) there) and the other five to round up to 3 (their errors are the \(e_{i,3}\) there), and then minimize
\[
M=\max\{\text{chosen }e_{i,2},\;\text{chosen }e_{j,3}\}.
\]
One checks that the two smallest \(e_{i,2}\) are at \(i=1,2\), namely 0.56 and 0.61.  If we make \(A_1=A_2=2\) and all the rest 3, then the largest error among the 3’s is
\(\max\{e_{3,3},\dots,e_{7,3}\}=0.35,\)
so
\[
M=\max(0.61,0.35)=0.61.
\]
No other choice of two 2’s gives a smaller maximum error.  Hence the minimal possible \(M\) is 0.61, and
\[
100M=61.
\]
\boxed{61}
